[[BLOCK:slide0_shape0]]
Disadvantages of Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide0_shape1]]
Yu Zhonghao

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape0]]
The Long History of Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape1]]
Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression), also known as Standard Least Squares Regression (SLS Regression), was discovered by Legendre (1805) and Gauss (1809) during the time of our ancestors.

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape0]]
Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape1]]
Least Squares = Minimization of Residual (残差 (Residual)) Squares  
Residual (残差 (Residual)) = The distance between actual and predicted values  
Best Fit

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape0]]
R-squared (R平方 (R Square))

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape1]]
The purpose of simple regression is to find a relationship.  
When multiple predictors are present, the multiple relationships are represented by R-squared (R平方 (R Square)) (explained variance).

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape0]]
Yes

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape1]]
X

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape2]]
R-squared (R平方 (R Square))

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape0]]
Ideal Situation

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape1]]
All predictors are orthogonal.

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape0]]
Inflated Variance Explanation

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape1]]
Imagine the overlapping area between Y and Xs represents the explained variance (multiple relationships).  
As you add more and more Xs to Y, the circle of Y is almost completely covered. R-squared (R Square) = 0.89! Amazing! A miracle! A gift from heaven!

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape0]]
Useless Model

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape1]]
A student asked me how to improve their grades. I told them that my fifty-variable regression model could predict nearly 89% of exam performance: studying for long hours, earning more money, buying a reliable car, watching less TV, going online more frequently, exercising more, attending church more often, praying more frequently, watching fewer movies, playing fewer video games, getting haircuts more often, drinking more milk and coffee... and so on.  
This complex model is utterly useless!

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression))

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape1]]
Lavery et al. (2016) stated: "Traditional methods (e.g., bivariate screening, stepwise selection, forward selection) have flaws. The statistics provided in regression outputs (p-values, parameter estimates, R-squared (R2), etc.) are developed for testing a specific model, and their application is unfounded when used repeatedly as in Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression))" (p. 1).

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression))

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape1]]
Smith (2018): "Many big data researchers believe that the more potential explanatory variables there are, the more useful Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression)) becomes in selecting explanatory variables. In reality, the more potential explanatory variables there are, the worse Stepwise Regression performs. Stepwise Regression does not solve the problem of too many explanatory variables in big data. Instead, big data exacerbates the flaws of Stepwise Regression."  
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6

[[BLOCK:slide8_note]]
Smith, G. 2018. Stay Away from Stepwise Regression (逐步回归 (Stepwise Regression)). Journal of Big Data. 5: 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6.

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape0]]
Goodness of Fit

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape1]]
In this example, I aim to predict weight using six variables from fitness data. The method used is Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression).

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape0]]
Negative Adjusted R-squared (Adjusted R-square)!

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape1]]
R-squared (R Square) is 0.199. Not bad! This model explains 20% of the variance in weight.  
However, when using multiple predictors, the program adjusts the inflated R-squared using Adjusted R-squared.  
The result is negative! R-squared is r * r and theoretically should not be negative. What is going on?

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape0]]
Negative R-squared (R平方 (R Square))

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape1]]
Zero indicates no explained variance.  
If a flat (horizontal) line is fitted to the data, it would explain nothing.  
If the model performs worse than a horizontal line and contradicts the data pattern, the Adjusted R-squared (R-squared) could be negative.  
Including too many predictors in the model may lead to worse performance than having no model at all.  
Analogy: Taking the wrong medication or a placebo when sick may result in worse outcomes than taking no medication.

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape0]]
Problems with Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression)

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape1]]
Excessive assumptions about Residuals (残差 (Residual)) and predictors  
The model tends to overfit the data.  
When some predictors are highly correlated (Multi-collinearity (多重共线性 (Multi-collinearity))), the model becomes unstable.  
No unique solution exists for large datasets.  
Regarding sample size, the traditional rule of thumb is 1 (predictor):10 (observations); if p > n, Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression) cannot run.  
The model must be linear.

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape0]]
Generalized Regression (广义回归 (Generalized Regression))

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape1]]
Proposed by Friedman (2008)  
Similar to abduction or IBE: do not fixate on a single answer but consider multiple possibilities.  
There may be multiple solutions to a problem, so why not explore different paths?  
Start with no model or zero coefficients.  
Try a range of models. The solution is flexible (variable).  
Choose the best solution (determined by the algorithm, not manual selection)!

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape0]]
Keep It Simple!

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape1]]
Also known as Regularized Regression (正则化回归 (Regularized Regression)): aims to reduce variance and improve model generalization to avoid overfitting.  
Also known as Penalized Regression (惩罚回归 (Penalized Regression)): penalizes overly complex or exaggeratedly fitted models → Keep It Simple, Stupid (KISS) principle!

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape0]]
Loss Function (损失函数 (Loss function))

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape1]]
A function that maps one or more variables' events to a real number, representing the cost of that event.  
What is the cost if an error occurs in the event? What will you lose?  
In statistics, this cost is the error or Residual (残差 (Residual)).

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape0]]
Loss Function (损失函数 (Loss function)): A Metaphor

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape1]]
A hiker tries to descend from a mountain at dusk. He surveys the surroundings, looking for all possible routes. As he proceeds along one path, he realizes it does not lead to his destination. He abandons this option because it wastes time and energy or incurs too high a cost. After several trials and errors, poor choices are eliminated, and the hiker gradually moves toward reducing losses. Eventually, he chooses the most cost-effective route, or a path that incurs negative costs.

[[BLOCK:slide18_shape0]]
Different Approaches to Regularization and Penalization

[[BLOCK:slide19_shape0]]
Regularization

[[BLOCK:slide19_shape1]]
The main difference between Ordinary Least Squares Regression (OLS Regression) and Penalized Regression (惩罚回归 (Penalized Regression)) is that Penalized Regression adds a regularization term to the Loss Function (损失函数 (Loss function)) to optimize coefficient estimation, hence Penalized Regression is also called Regularized Regression (正则化回归 (Regularized Regression)).  
L1 Regularization (L1正则化 (L1 Regularization)) (LASSO): uses the sum of the absolute values of coefficient estimates as the regularization term.  
L2 Regularization (L2正则化 (L2 Regularization)) (Ridge Regression (岭回归 (Ridge))): uses the sum of the squared values of coefficient estimates as the regularization term.
